title: First Five-Year Economic Development Plan
description: Park Chung-hee government launches state-led, export-oriented industrialization planning, the model for Korea's subsequent development plans.
source_name: Wikipedia — Miracle on the Han River (cross-check chronology skeleton)

title: Korea-Japan Normalization Treaty
description: Diplomatic normalization brings Japanese reparations/loans that helped fund industrial development.

title: Heavy and Chemical Industry (HCI) Drive
description: State-directed push into steel, shipbuilding, chemicals and electronics via subsidized credit to designated conglomerates (chaebols).

title: Assassination of Park Chung-hee
description: End of the Park era; economic policy shifts toward the Chun Doo-hwan government's stabilization and liberalization program.

title: 1988 Seoul Olympics
description: Games showcase Korea's industrial transformation and accelerate financial-market opening.

title: OECD accession
description: South Korea joins the OECD, accelerating capital-account liberalization shortly before the 1997 crisis.

title: Asian Financial Crisis — IMF request
description: Government formally requests IMF standby assistance amid a won collapse and capital flight.

title: IMF $58.4bn bailout agreed
description: Largest IMF rescue package to that date (IMF $21bn, World Bank $10bn, ADB $4bn, bilateral $23.4bn), conditioned on financial-sector restructuring, fiscal tightening, and labor-market flexibility reforms.
source_name: Wikipedia — 1997 Asian Financial Crisis (cross-check; standard, uncontested figures)

title: Early IMF loan repayment
description: Korea repays its IMF loans three years ahead of schedule, symbolically closing the crisis chapter.

title: Global Financial Crisis impact
description: Won depreciates sharply; Korea deploys large fiscal stimulus and a currency swap line with the US Federal Reserve.

title: COVID-19 first case and economic response
description: Korea's early, widely-studied testing-and-tracing response limits the need for a full lockdown, cushioning the growth shock relative to peers.

title: Korean War begins
description: North Korean invasion triggers a three-year war fought across the peninsula; the conflict kills roughly 1.5 million people and destroys about a quarter of South Korea's capital stock, leaving early-1960s per-capita GDP near $100.
source_name: Wikipedia — Aftermath of the Korean War (cross-check)

title: First currency reform — won redenominated as hwan
description: Bank of Korea exchanges the won for a new hwan at 100:1 to curb wartime inflation, the third currency reform since liberation.

title: Park Chung-hee's May 16 coup
description: Major General Park Chung-hee leads a military coup against the Second Republic amid economic instability (23%+ unemployment) and political factionalism, dissolving the National Assembly and installing the Supreme Council for National Reconstruction that launches state-led export-oriented industrialization.

title: Second currency reform — hwan redenominated as won
description: Bank of Korea reintroduces the won at 10 hwan = 1 won to support the new Five-Year Plan and channel hoarded wealth into industrial investment; this won remains South Korea's currency today.

title: Saemaul Undong (New Community Movement) launched
description: Park Chung-hee launches a state-directed rural modernization campaign combining infrastructure grants, cooperative labor and productivity incentives, credited with narrowing the urban-rural income gap during the industrialization drive.

title: August 3 Measure — corporate curb-market debt frozen
description: Presidential Emergency Decree freezes all private (curb-market) debts owed by companies to informal lenders and converts them into long-term low-interest obligations, a bailout of debt-laden chaebol ahead of the Heavy and Chemical Industry drive.

title: Won devalued amid first negative-growth recession
description: Second oil shock, poor harvest and political turmoil after Park's assassination push the won down 20% and the economy to its first contraction since the industrialization drive began, prompting a stabilization and liberalization pivot under Chun Doo-hwan.

title: Real-Name Financial Transaction System introduced
description: President Kim Young-sam bans anonymous and borrowed-name financial accounts by emergency presidential order, targeting an estimated $15bn hidden from taxation through pseudonymous accounts and modernizing financial-sector transparency.

title: Daewoo Group collapses
description: Daewoo, South Korea's second-largest chaebol, is declared bankrupt with roughly $50-80bn in debt — the largest corporate collapse in Korean history — after taking on debt through the Asian Financial Crisis rather than deleveraging, later revealed to involve $43bn in accounting fraud.
source_name: Wikipedia — Daewoo dissolution and corruption scandal (cross-check)

title: Credit card crisis
description: Post-crisis deregulation of consumer credit fuels a household-debt bubble; by end-2003 roughly 4 million Koreans (near 10% of the population) have defaulted on credit-card debt and LG Card collapses, forcing a government-led bailout.
source_name: Bank for International Settlements — BIS Papers No. 46

title: Korea-China Free Trade Agreement takes effect
description: Bilateral FTA with Korea's largest trading partner enters into force, eliminating tariffs on the large majority of goods traded between the two economies.
source_name: Wikipedia — China–South Korea Free Trade Agreement (cross-check)
